paperTitle: JetPrism: diagnosing convergence for generative simulation and inverse problems in nuclear physics
abstract: High-fidelity Monte Carlo simulations and complex inverse problems, such as mapping smeared experimental observations to ground-truth states, are computationally intensive yet essential for robust data analysis. Conditional Flow Matching (CFM) offers a mathematically robust approach to accelerating these tasks, but we demonstrate its standard training loss is fundamentally misleading. In rigorous physics applications, CFM loss plateaus prematurely, serving as an unreliable indicator of true convergence and physical fidelity. To investigate this disconnect, we designed JetPrism, a configurable CFM framework acting as an efficient generative surrogate for evaluating unconditional generation and conditional detector unfolding. Using synthetic stress tests and a Jefferson Lab kinematic dataset ($\gamma p \to \rho^0 p \to \pi^+\pi^- p$) relevant to the forthcoming Electron-Ion Collider (EIC), we establish that physics-informed metrics continue to improve significantly long after the standard loss converges. Consequently, we propose a multi-metric evaluation protocol incorporating marginal and pairwise $\chi^2$ statistics, $W_1$ distances, correlation matrix distances ($D_{\mathrm{corr}}$), and nearest-neighbor distance ratios ($R_{\mathrm{NN}}$). By demonstrating that domain-specific evaluations must supersede generic loss metrics, this work establishes JetPrism as a dependable generative surrogate that ensures precise statistical agreement with ground-truth data without memorizing the training set. While demonstrated in nuclear physics, this diagnostic framework is readily extensible to parameter generation and complex inverse problems across broad domains. Potential applications span medical imaging, astrophysics, semiconductor discovery, and quantitative finance, where high-fidelity simulation, rigorous inversion, and generative reliability are critical.

chunks.text: High-fidelity Monte Carlo simulations and complex inverse problems, such as mapping smeared experimental observations to ground-truth states, are computationally intensive yet essential for robust data analysis. Conditional Flow Matching (CFM) offers a mathematically robust approach to accelerating these tasks, but we demonstrate its standard training loss is fundamentally misleading. In rigorous physics applications, CFM loss plateaus prematurely, serving as an unreliable indicator of true convergence and physical fidelity. To investigate this disconnect, we designed JetPrism, a configurable CFM framework acting as an efficient generative surrogate for evaluating unconditional generation and conditional detector unfolding. Using synthetic stress tests and a Jefferson Lab kinematic dataset ($\gamma p \to \rho^0 p \to \pi^+\pi^- p$) relevant to the forthcoming Electron-Ion Collider (EIC), we establish that physics-informed metrics continue to improve significantly long after the standard loss converges. Consequently, we propose a multi-metric evaluation protocol incorporating marginal and pairwise $\chi^2$ statistics, $W_1$ distances, correlation matrix distances ($D_{\mathrm{corr}}$), and nearest-neighbor distance ratios ($R_{\mathrm{NN}}$). By demonstrating that domain-specific evaluations must supersede generic loss metrics, this work establishes JetPrism as a dependable generative surrogate that ensures precise statistical agreement with ground-truth data without memorizing the training set. While demonstrated in nuclear physics, this diagnostic framework is readily extensible to parameter generation and complex inverse problems across broad domains. Potential applications span medical imaging, astrophysics, semiconductor discovery, and quantitative finance, where high-fidelity simulation, rigorous inversion, and generative reliability are critical.

Keywords: Simulation methods and programs, analysis and statistical methods, software architec-
                                        tures, data processing methods
                                          1 Corresponding author.
 1    Introduction

Experimental nuclear and particle physics analyses require massive simulation datasets to populate
phase space. While raw Monte Carlo generation is computationally inexpensive per event, the
required volume imposes a significant computational burden. For Jefferson Lab experiments
(e.g., CLAS12, GlueX), modeling complex particle transport, secondary interactions, and detector
responses makes GEANT-based detector simulation a primary computational bottleneck.
     Detector unfolding—recovering true event-level observables from smeared measurements—relies
heavily on these detailed simulations to determine precise detector responses and acceptance correc-
tions. The immense computational demands of this process motivate developing AI-based surrogate
models that approximate traditional simulations at a fraction of the cost.
     Deep generative models, particularly Conditional Flow Matching (

1. Convergence diagnostics and validation suite. We identify the premature plateau of standard
       CFM loss, which obscures ongoing physical refinement, and establish a rigorous multi-metric
       protocol (𝜒2 , 𝑊1 , 𝐷 corr , NFE, 𝑅NN ) to accurately track true convergence, verify generative
       fidelity, and prevent data memorization.

2. JetPrism Framework. We introduce JetPrism, a configurable CFM-based tool, and validate
       its capabilities for event generation and conditional detector unfolding on a realistic Jefferson
       Lab dataset (𝛾 𝑝 → 𝜌 0 𝑝 → 𝜋 + 𝜋 − 𝑝) relevant to the forthcoming Electron-Ion Collider (EIC).

3. Synthetic stress tests. We provide controlled 1D benchmarks (gaussian, high-cut, multi-
       peak, high-frequency, delta, uniform, and exponential) to isolate generative capa-
       bilities and diagnose topological failure modes prior to deployment on real physics data.

2    Related work

Detector unfolding is a cornerstone High-Energy Physics (HEP) challenge [1]. Traditional binned
techniques like Iterative Bayesian Unfolding (IBU) routinely suffer from dimensionality curses.
OmniFold [2] overcame these limitations via unbinned neural classifiers, enabling simultaneous
high-dimensional unfolding [3]. The field subsequently shifted toward deep generative sampling [4].
While Generative Adversarial Networks (GANs) can recover true distributions [5], they remain
hard to converge. Continuous Normalizing Flows (CNFs) and diffusion models [6, 7] improve
scalability but demand costly Ordinary Differential Equation (ODE) evaluations. Conditional Flow
Matching [8] bypasses this via a simulation-free regression objective, further refined by Optimal
Transport Conditional Flow Matching (OT-CFM) [9] and physics-constrained variants [10–12].
     These foundations rapidly populate HEP

–2–
 architectures yield analysis-ready generative unfolding [16, 17] for complex final states [18, 19].
While generative Artificial Intelligence (AI) is vital for EIC simulations [20, 21], current imple-
mentations often assume generic metrics safely indicate modeling quality. A crucial gap remains:
to our knowledge, no prior work systematically addresses the disconnect between training loss
convergence and true kinematic fidelity of CFM model in HEP. We fill this gap with JetPrism.
By coupling a stable CFM architecture with a strict physics-informed validation protocol (𝜒2 , 𝑊1 ,
𝐷 corr ), JetPrism overcomes flow optimization blindness, providing a reproducible tool expressly
designed to verify physical convergence.

3     Methodology

3.1    Datasets and feature representation
MC-POM dataset. The MC-POM (Monte Carlo Pomeron) dataset models exclusive photopro-
duction 𝛾 𝑝 → 𝜌 0 𝑝 → 𝜋 + 𝜋 − 𝑝. We focus on forward kinematics with low momentum transfer
(|𝑡| < 1 GeV2 ), where pomeron exchange dominates. In this regime, the P-wave 𝜌(770) resonance
is prominent in the 𝑀 (𝜋 + 𝜋 − ) mass spectrum. We utilize a fixed dataset of 8,000,000 events.
       Events initially consist of 24-dimensional vectors encoding the four-momenta of all involved
particles (𝑝 𝛾 , 𝑝 1 , 𝑝 2 , 𝜋 ± ) and derived variables (𝑡, 𝑀 𝜋 𝜋 , cos 𝜃, 𝜙) evaluated in the 𝜋 + 𝜋 − helicity rest
                  𝜇   𝜇    𝜇

frame. To eliminate redundancy, we project the data into a 10-dimensional phase space. Exclud-
ing the recoil proton (fixed by four-momentum conservation) and extracting the spatial momenta
( 𝑝 𝑥 , 𝑝 𝑦 , 𝑝 𝑧 ) of the remaining four particles yields 12 components. Dropping the identically zero
𝑝 𝑦 components of the incident photon and target proton results in the final 10 dimensions. Each
feature was standardized to zero mean and unit variance, and subsequently scaled by a factor of
5.0. Empirically, this specific scaling factor yielded marginal performance improvements compared
to baseline factors of 1.0 and 2.0. This deliberate mismatch with the unit-variance prior N (0, 𝐼)
separates source and target supports along 𝑥 𝑡 = 𝑡 𝑥 1 + (1−𝑡) 𝑥0 , amplifying the deterministic velocity
signal and improving training stability. The inverse transform recovers physical units.
       For detector un

Synthetic mock datasets. To isolate modeling challenges, we construct a diverse suite of 1D
synthetic benchmarks (gaussian, high-cut, multi-peak, high-frequency, delta, uniform,
and exponential) across configuration presets. These controlled environments enable rigorous
ablation studies of mode collapse and fine-grained resolution prior to real physics deployment.
Detailed formulations and extended results are in Appendix C.

Figure 1. An illustration of the CFM generation process. The model learns to transform a simple base
Gaussian distribution (left) to a complex three-peak mixed-Gaussian target distribution (right) by learning
the intermediate velocity field (middle).

Flow Matching (FM) defines the objective of learning a time-dependent velocity field 𝑣 𝜃 (𝑥, 𝑡)
whose induced ODE transports samples from a simple prior 𝑝 0 = N (0, 𝐼) to the target data
distribution 𝑝 1 . Because the marginal FM objective is intractable, we employ Conditional Flow
Matching (CFM) [8]. This approach conditions on individual data samples to construct simple
probability paths, yielding a tractable regression loss that recovers the exact marginal velocity field.
     Given a target data sample 𝑥 1 ∼ 𝑝 data and noise 𝑥0 ∼ N (0, 𝐼), CFM constructs interpolated
samples along a linear conditional path:

𝑥 𝑡 = (1 − 𝑡)𝑥 0 + 𝑡𝑥 1 ,      𝑡 ∈ [0, 1].                              (3.1)

The conditional vector field generating this path is the constant derivative: 𝑢 𝑡 = 𝑑𝑑𝑡𝑥𝑡 = 𝑥1 − 𝑥 0 . The
CFM loss then trains a neural network to regress against this target velocity:

LCFM (𝜃) = E𝑡 , 𝑥0 , 𝑥1 ∥𝑣 𝜃 (𝑥 𝑡 , 𝑡) − (𝑥 1 − 𝑥 0 ) ∥ 2 .
                                                                                            
                                                                                                                        (3.2)

An intuitive visual representation of this learned velocity mapping, transporting samples from a base
Gaussian noise state through intermediate trajectories toward a multi-modal deterministic target, is
provided in Figure 1.
    We use two distinct network variants under this shared CFM objective:

Unconditional generation network. For generative simulation, we train an unconditional velocity
network 𝑣 𝜃 (𝑥 𝑡 , 𝑡) that takes as input the current state 𝑥 𝑡 concatenated with a Fourier embedding of
𝑡, autonomously mapping pure Gaussian noise into the target physics distribution.

–4–
 Conditional unfolding network. For detector unfolding, we train a conditional velocity network
𝑣 𝜃 (𝑥 𝑡 , 𝑡 | 𝑐) parameterized by the detector-level measurement 𝑐. The conditioning vector 𝑐 is
processed through a learned two-layer SiLU-activated Multi-Layer Perceptron (MLP) and con-
catenated alongside the 𝑥 𝑡 state and time embedding. During inference, this condition 𝑐 is held
constant throughout the entire ODE integration, allowing the network to deterministically recover
particle-level kinematics from localized, smeared observations.
      Both variants utilize residual backbone architectures [22] with SiLU activations. At inference,
generation is performed by integrating the learned ODE from 𝑡 = 0 to 𝑡 = 1 via an adaptive
Dormand-Prince solver using deterministic paths, establishing a deterministic mapping between
the prior and target distributions. Comprehensive hyperparameter

3.3     Physics-informed metrics
To rigorously evaluate model performance, we monitor the following physics-informed metrics in
addition to standard training metrics, such as loss, during the validation and testing phases:

1. Marginals: We report the 𝜒2 statistic and the Wasserstein-1 distance (𝑊1 ) between generated
         and true univariate distributions.

2. Pairwise joints: We report the 2D binned 𝜒2 statistic (𝜒2D
                                                                 2 ) over all feature pairs, testing

whether the model captures bivariate dependencies beyond individual marginals.

3. Global correlation structure: We report the correlation matrix distance 𝐷 corr = ∥corr(truth)−
         corr(gen) ∥ 𝐹 , the Frobenius norm of the Pearson correlation-matrix difference, measuring the
         holistic reproduction of linear dependencies across all channels.

4. Memorization: We report the nearest-neighbor distance ratio 𝑅NN = 𝑑¯gen→train /𝑑¯train→train ,
         where 𝑑¯ denotes the mean 𝐿 2 nearest-neighbor distance. A ratio 𝑅NN ≈ 1 indicates general-
         ization, whereas 𝑅NN ≪ 1 flags memorization.

In tabular summaries, we present 𝜒2 , 𝑊1 , 𝜒2D  2 ,𝐷
                                                         corr , and 𝑅NN . For dynamic tracking (figures),
we monitor training loss, Number of Function Evaluations (NFE) (lower values indicate straighter
flows for adaptive solvers), 𝑊1 , and 𝐷 corr . Detailed mathematical formulations are in Appendix B.

4      Results

4.1     Synthetic benchmark validation
To isolate modeling challenges, we first validated the architecture on synthetic 1D distributions;
detailed results with complex topologies are provided in Appendix C.

4.2     Performance on MC-POM dataset
A crucial observation during MC-POM training is that the convergence of the standard CFM
velocity loss does not align with true physical fidelity. As Figure 2 shows, the CFM loss plateaus
rapidly after ∼25 epochs. In contrast, physics-informed metrics (𝑊1 , 𝐷 corr ) improve steadily until

–5–
 epoch 500. Additionally, the NFE stabilizes around epoch 600, indicating extended training is
required to find stable probability paths, as adaptive ODE solvers require fewer steps (NFE) when
the learned velocity field becomes straighter. Thus, CFM loss alone cannot guarantee accurate
kinematic reconstruction, necessitating decoupled physical validation metrics.

5                Discussion and conclusion

For practitioners applying generative models to HEP, our results underscore a critical training
dynamic: the convergence of the standard CFM velocity loss can be misleading and does not
ensure the fidelity of physical observables. Because physical distributions require significantly
longer training to stabilize, decoupled evaluation using domain-specific observables is essential.
Accounting for this discrepancy, we demonstrated through JetPrism—validated on synthetic and
JLab MC-POM datasets—that CFM provides a robust, deterministic framework for capturing
complex phase spaces and unfolding detector-level kinematics.
    By learning flexible mappings between detector observables and particle truths, JetPrism offers
an AI-driven, unbinned alternative to computationally expensive Monte Carlo simulations. This

–8–
 event-level approach reduces binning artifacts and facilitates exploring low cross-section topologies
(e.g., near-threshold 𝐽/𝜓 photoproduction, 𝜙 SDMEs, exotic hybrid mesons). This accelerated
pipeline is highly advantageous for EIC development, enabling rapid systematic iterations of de-
tector configurations without repeated simulation campaigns.A detailed quantification of these
computational throughput gains is provided in Appendix F.
     JetPrism prioritizes modularity and reproducibility using a PyTorch Lightning [23], Hydra [24],
and Weights & Biases [25] backend. We utilized standard CFM, which achieves high-fidelity
generation and unfolding across joint and marginal distributions without the high computational
costs of calculating optimal pairings via OT-CFM on large datasets.
     Beyond nuclear physics, this methodology naturally extends to other scientific fields tackl

and Zenodo (https://doi.org/10.5281/zenodo.19277778, https://doi.org/10.5281/

A   Network architecture and training details

Building upon the methodology in Section 3.2, both network variants share a residual MLP ar-
chitecture. Table 2 lists the primary hyperparameters. The unconditional network evaluates the
concatenated state [𝑥 𝑡 ∥ 𝑒 𝑡 ] ∈ R𝐷+𝑑𝑡 , where 𝑒 𝑡 is the Fourier time embedding. For the conditional
variant, the detector measurement 𝑐 ∈ R𝐷 is embedded into 𝑒 𝑐 ∈ R128 , expanding the input to
[𝑥 𝑡 ∥ 𝑒 𝑡 ∥ 𝑒 𝑐 ] ∈ R𝐷+𝑑𝑡 +128 .

Table 2. Network architecture and training hyperparameters.
    Architecture               Value                  Training        Value
    Input dimension 𝐷          10                     Optimizer       AdamW
    Hidden dimensions          [512] 6 (6 layers)     Learning rate   10−4 (decay 0.5, patience 50)
    Time embedding dim.        64                     Weight decay    10−5
    Activation function        SiLU                   Batch size      20,000
    Total parameters           ∼2.7M                  Total epochs    800

Time conditioning. The scalar time 𝑡 ∈ [0, 1] is embedded using 32 geometrically-spaced fre-
quencies up to a maximum frequency 𝜔max = 64. A learned linear projection maps this Fourier

– 11 –
 feature into a R64 vector, providing the network with high-frequency temporal components necessary
for resolving sharp changes in the velocity field.

Residual blocks. The core backbone comprises five residual blocks. Each block applies a two-
                                                              
layer SiLU-activated sequence, SiLU Linear(SiLU(Linear(ℎ))) + ℎ, promoting stable gradient
flow and facilitating the learning of identity mappings where the vector field is approximately
constant.

Optimization details. We optimize parameters with AdamW. A ReduceLROnPlateau scheduler
tracks the epoch-level validation CFM loss (val/loss_epoch), decaying the initial 10−4 learning
rate by a factor of 0.5 upon detecting plateaus over a 50-epoch patience threshold (capped minimally
at 10−7 ). For efficiency, validation metrics are computed over a random subset of 50,000 events.
Instead, the globally optimal checkpoint persisting into final tabular summaries monitors the pure
generative physical reconstruction (val/chi2_mean) over the raw CFM velocity loss, as discussed
in detail throughout Section 4.2.

Generation and inference details. For the Dormand-Prince (DOPRI5) ODE solver introduced in
Section 3.2, absolute and relative tolerances are uniformly set to 10−7 for unconditional generation
and relaxed to 10−3 for unfolding tasks. On a single NVIDIA Tesla V100-SXM2 GPU, one training
iteration on a 20,000-event batch completes in ∼32 ms (∼630K samples/s). Inference throughput
reaches ∼2.6K samples/s for strict unconditional generation and ∼71.7K samples/s for conditional
unfolding, with a detailed breakdown provided in Section F.

Implementation and environment details. The JetPrism framework is implemented in Python
(≥3.13) utilizing PyTorch (2.10.0) and PyTorch Lightning (2.6.1) [23] for hardware-agnostic train-
ing. Core underlying dependencies include torchdyn (1.0.6) for numerical ODE integration.
     All model training and computational evaluations were primarily executed on the UVA Rivanna
High-Performance Computing Cluster. GPU acceleration was performed using a single NVIDIA
Tesla V100-SXM2 tensor core GPU (32 GB VRAM) and an NVIDIA A100-SXM4 GPU (40 GB
VRAM) with CUDA 12.8. CPU-based inference and data processing were performed on an Intel(R)
Xeon(R) Gold 6230 CPU @ 2.10GHz.

B   Evaluation metrics

To rigorously assess the fidelity of the generated kinematic distributions against the ground truth, we
utilize a structured set of evaluation metrics spanning marginal distributions, multivariate structures,
and network memorization characteristics:

Marginals. The quality of individual feature distributions is measured primarily using the 𝜒2
statistic and the Wasserstein-1 distance (𝑊1 ):

• Mean 𝜒2 (val/chi2_mean): Evaluated over 1D binned histograms of individual features.
      To prevent artifacts from outlier limits, exactly 50 uniform bins are dynamically bound
      between the minimum and maximum values of the true expected distribution. The generated

– 12 –
       histograms are normalized to match the total event count of the truth distribution before
      evaluation. Formally, for a single feature, the statistic is:
                                                  50
                                                  ∑︁ (𝑂 𝑖 − 𝐸 𝑖 ) 2
                                           𝜒2 =                                                (B.1)
                                                  𝑖=1
                                                              𝐸𝑖

where 𝑂 𝑖 is the normalized generated count and 𝐸 𝑖 is the expected true count in the 𝑖-th bin.

• Mean Wasserstein distance (val/wasserstein_mean): Measures the minimum mass-
      transport distance required to transform the generated 1D marginal distributions to the true
      distributions. To explicitly circumvent the manual binning limits of 𝜒2 , this metric evaluates
      the raw, unbinned 1D distributions via their cumulative distribution functions (CDFs) 𝐹 (𝑥)
      and 𝐺 (𝑥):                             ∫    ∞
                                      𝑊1 =            |𝐹 (𝑥) − 𝐺 (𝑥)| 𝑑𝑥                       (B.2)
                                              −∞

Pairwise joints. To evaluate bivariate dependencies between kinematic variables, we examine
two-dimensional distributions:

• Mean 2D 𝜒2 (val/chi2_2d_mean): Extends the 𝜒2 formulation to two-dimensional cross-
      sections across all 45 unique pairwise feature combinations, returning the arithmetic mean.
      Each axis is divided into 20 uniform bins (yielding exactly 400 rectangular bins per pair),
      determined entirely by the ground-truth coordinate extremes. This rigorously tests whether
      the generator captures underlying multivariate dependencies beyond independent marginals.

Global correlation structure. To verify that generated events accurately reproduce multi-dimensional
kinematic constraints, we assess holistic structural fidelity using:

• Correlation matrix distance 𝐷 corr (val/correlation_distance): A holistic measure of
      how effectively the network reconstructs global linear relationships. It is evaluated as the
      Frobenius norm of the difference between the sample Pearson correlation matrices:

𝐷 corr = ∥corr(truth) − corr(gen) ∥ 𝐹                       (B.3)

Memorization.     We test for generative generalization using comparative 𝐿 2 nearest-neighbor
mappings:

• Nearest-neighbor distance ratio 𝑅NN (nn/memorization_ratio): Serves as our principal
      guard against model over-fitting. It calculates the ratio of the mean nearest-neighbor distance
      from generated samples to the training set (𝑑¯gen→train ) versus the mean nearest-neighbor
      distance natively found within the training set itself (𝑑¯train→train ):
                                                         𝑑¯gen→train
                                            𝑅NN =                                              (B.4)
                                                        𝑑¯train→train
      A ratio 𝑅NN ≈ 1 indicates high-fidelity generalization, whereas 𝑅NN ≪ 1 flags strict dataset
      memorization. Notably, for certain synthetic mock datasets, the native distance 𝑑¯train→train
      can be exceptionally small, causing 𝑅NN to appear abnormally large; this is an expected

– 13 –
     Additional auxiliary scalars monitored behind the 𝑅NN evaluation include:

• nn/D_gen_to_train_mean: The arithmetic mean 𝐿 2 Euclidean distance from generated
      events to their single closest element in the true training manifold.

• nn/D_train_to_train_mean: The native baseline mean 𝐿 2 distance computed by match-
      ing points from a random sub-sample of the training set to the remainder of the training
      elements.

• nn/D_gen_to_train_min and nn/D_train_to_train_min: The corresponding absolute
      minimum scalar values computed for the distributions above.

C   Synthetic benchmark analysis

To isolate and understand the fundamental generative capabilities of the architecture independent of
physical kinematics, we conducted extensive evaluations on a suite of synthetic 1D mock datasets.
These benchmarks are specifically designed to stress-test the model against complex topological
structures commonly encountered in physics, such as multi-modal overlapping resonances, sharp
kinematic cut-offs, and high-frequency perturbative noise.
      Table 3 summarizes the quantitative generation fidelity and memorization metrics across these
diverse 1D topologies. Across the majority of datasets, the model achieves exceptionally low
Wasserstein (𝑊1 ) distances and minimal 𝜒2 deviations, indicating robust macro-level distribution
reconstruction. Crucially, the nearest-neighbor distance tracking parameters confirm that the frame-
work successfully generalizes without merely duplicating the

However, perfectly resolving these dense spatial features fundamentally requires decoupled val-
idation schemas. Common physical modeling failure modes before full convergence are depicted
in Figure 13, revealing the inherent edge-case sensitivities of flow-based generation. When under-
trained, the generated manifolds frequently exhibit smeared mass boundaries along strict kinematic
limits, or they produce spurious, bridged population points between completely separate disjoint
peaks. Figure 14 actively tracks the resolution of these artifacts by charting the progressive struc-
tural refinement over the different training stages. These sequential density profiles underscore that
while the general macroscopic envelope of the distribution is identified rapidly, extended parameter
optimization is strictly required for the velocity field to harden and reliably resolve targeted physical
nuan

D     Extended unconditional generation validation

Beyond analyzing individual 1D marginal distributions, predicting complex multivariate correla-
tions is crucial for validating physical simulations. Figure 15 presents the comparative Pearson cor-
relation matrices for both the generated and ground-truth kinematics in the unconditional MC-POM
generation task. The high degree of concordance across the entire parameter space demonstrates
the model’s capacity to naturally reconstruct global linear relationships and couple interdependent
physical limits, confirming high-fidelity multidimensional learning.

V100-SXM2 or an NVIDIA A100-SXM4 GPU, while the CPU evaluation is performed on an
Intel(R) Xeon(R) Gold 6230 Processor. The V100 GPU delivers a ∼25× speedup over the CPU
in both phases and tasks, and the A100 further accelerates this, reaching throughputs exceeding
880K samples/s during training. These significant performance gains enable the high-throughput
generation characteristic of specialized fast simulation workflows.
     While training throughput is comparable across both tasks, inference speed differs dramati-
cally: conditional unfolding achieves substantially higher throughput than unconditional generation.
On the A100, unfolding operates at 1.01 × 105 events/s compared to 3.70 × 103 events/s for un-
conditional generation (an ∼27× speed disparity consistent across GPU hardware). This disparity
arises primarily from the ODE solver tolerances: unconditional generation uses str
